skill.name: deepchem
skill.description: Guides molecular machine learning workflows with DeepChem, including featurization, MoleculeNet benchmarks, property prediction, graph neural networks, and transfer learning.
skill.summary: Build and evaluate DeepChem models for molecular property prediction and drug discovery research.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many severe alerts, but review found most are false positives from markdown code fences and scientific terminology such as molecular fingerprints, SAM alignment files, and GAN models. The skill is not malicious, but it includes executable Python scripts that read user-selected datasets and may trigger DeepChem or Hugging Face downloads, so publication should include an operational warning.

medium_findings.title: Executable ML Scripts Read Local Data and May Download Assets
medium_findings.description: The included Python scripts load user-provided CSV files and named MoleculeNet or pretrained model assets. This is legitimate for DeepChem workflows, but it creates filesystem and network exposure if run on sensitive data or unrestricted networks. Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE for operational risk, not malicious behavior. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The scripts directly call CSVLoader.create_dataset on user paths and call DeepChem dataset/model loaders; no credential access or shell execution was found.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts clearly read local datasets and use DeepChem or Hugging Face model loading paths. The behavior matches the skill purpose, so the concern is controlled execution context rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts point to markdown code fences showing how users can run scripts or install DeepChem. They are documentation examples, not code that the skill automatically executes. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for autonomous command execution. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The cited lines are fenced bash snippets inside SKILL.md, and the Python scripts do not call subprocess, os.system, eval, or shell backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is markdown documentation showing commands for the user to run. No automatic command execution path was found in the reviewed scripts.

low_findings.title: Scientific Terms Misclassified as Cryptography, C2, or Sensitive Files
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, C2, SAM database, and certificate/key alerts are false positives caused by domain terms. Examples include molecular fingerprints, SAM/BAM/CRAM sequence alignment loaders, GAN model names, model keys in dictionaries, and task keywords. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Confidence: 0.96. Confidence reasoning: The cited terms occur in chemistry and bioinformatics API documentation or normal Python dictionaries, not in credential handling, C2 behavior, or cryptographic code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is scientific machine learning. The suspicious tokens are model names, file-format names, or molecular descriptor terminology rather than security-sensitive operations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Public Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The skill links to DeepChem documentation, the DeepChem GitHub repository, and tutorials. These URLs are user-facing references and do not transmit local data by themselves. Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for exfiltration risk. Confidence: 0.91. Confidence reasoning: The URLs are static documentation links in markdown, and no code was found that posts secrets or user data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links are public reference resources at expected DeepChem domains. They are not used in executable code paths for data transfer.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Dataset Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts accept dataset paths from CLI arguments and pass them to DeepChem loaders. This is expected for ML tooling, but users should run the scripts only on trusted local files and avoid sensitive datasets unless they understand downstream processing. Confidence: 0.82. Confidence reasoning: The path flow is direct and visible, but it is a normal feature for model training scripts rather than a hidden access pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CLI arguments clearly allow local file paths. There is no evidence of broad filesystem scanning or secret file targeting.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-Dependent Dataset and Model Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: Some examples load MoleculeNet datasets or pretrained model identifiers that may require network downloads through DeepChem or Hugging Face. This should be disclosed because it may contact external package or model services. Confidence: 0.80. Confidence reasoning: The code references known dataset loaders and model IDs, but actual network behavior depends on installed library cache and runtime configuration.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: DeepChem and Hugging Face model loading commonly download assets. The code does not include custom network exfiltration endpoints.

content.user_title: Build Molecular ML Models with DeepChem
content.value_statement: Molecular machine learning requires specialized data handling, featurization, and evaluation choices. This skill provides DeepChem workflows for property prediction, graph neural networks, benchmarks, and transfer learning.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to load molecular datasets from CSV, SDF, FASTA, and MoleculeNet sources.
Recommends DeepChem featurizers for fingerprints, descriptors, graph models, sequence models, and 3D structures.
Provides workflows for solubility, toxicity, ADMET, materials, protein, and molecule generation tasks.
Includes scripts for solubility prediction, graph neural network training, and transfer learning experiments.
Shows evaluation patterns using scaffold splits, normalization, ROC-AUC, R2, MAE, and RMSE metrics.
Documents DeepChem model families, including GCN, GAT, MPNN, ChemBERTa, GROVER, and MolFormer.
limitations: The included scripts are examples and require a working DeepChem environment with compatible dependencies.
Some dataset and pretrained model workflows may download external assets at runtime.
The skill does not validate scientific correctness of input data or generated molecular predictions.
Large GNN and transfer learning workflows may need GPU resources and careful hyperparameter tuning.

use_cases.title: Prototype ADMET Prediction
use_cases.description: Train a baseline model from SMILES data, split by molecular scaffold, and compare prediction metrics before deeper experiments.

use_cases.title: Benchmark Molecular Models
use_cases.description: Run MoleculeNet datasets with fingerprint, graph, and pretrained model workflows to compare model families under consistent metrics.

use_cases.title: Prepare Teaching Examples
use_cases.description: Use the reference workflows to demonstrate featurization choices, data leakage risks, and model evaluation for chemical datasets.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Featurizer
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a molecular property task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the deepchem skill to recommend a featurizer for my dataset. I have [number] molecules, SMILES strings, and a [classification or regression] target.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Baseline Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the deepchem skill to outline a baseline property prediction workflow for [target property]. Include loading, splitting, featurization, model choice, and metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Compare GNN Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the deepchem skill to compare GCN, GAT, MPNN, and AttentiveFP for my [dataset size] molecular dataset. Explain tradeoffs and expected requirements.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Transfer Learning
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow for small or specialized datasets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the deepchem skill to design a transfer learning experiment with ChemBERTa or GROVER for [task]. Include data preparation, scaffold split, metrics, and overfitting controls.

output_examples.input: I have 800 molecules and want to predict aqueous solubility from SMILES.
output: Use a simple fingerprint baseline first because the dataset is small.
Use scaffold splitting to reduce leakage from similar molecular structures.
Report R2, MAE, and RMSE on train, validation, and test splits.
Consider transfer learning only after the baseline exposes the error profile.

output_examples.input: I want to benchmark a graph neural network on Tox21.
output: Load Tox21 through MoleculeNet with graph featurization and scaffold splitting.
Start with GCN as the baseline, then compare GAT or MPNN if resources allow.
Use ROC-AUC as the primary metric because Tox21 is a multi-task classification dataset.

output_examples.input: My custom molecular model performs well on random splits but poorly in deployment.
output: Check for molecular data leakage caused by random splitting.
Re-run evaluation with ScaffoldSplitter or a diversity-based split.
Compare train, validation, and test scores to identify overfitting.

best_practices: Use scaffold splitting for drug discovery datasets unless you have a clear reason to use another split.
Start with a fingerprint or descriptor baseline before using graph neural networks or transfer learning.
Run scripts in an isolated environment and review network downloads before using proprietary datasets.
anti_patterns: Do not report random split metrics as deployment-ready evidence for molecular discovery tasks.
Do not use large GNN models on tiny datasets before establishing a simpler baseline.
Do not run pretrained model workflows on restricted networks without checking download behavior.

faq.question: What is this skill for?
faq.answer: It helps users design and run DeepChem workflows for molecular property prediction, benchmarks, GNNs, and transfer learning.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes Python example scripts for solubility prediction, graph neural networks, and transfer learning.

faq.question: Can it work with custom datasets?
faq.answer: Yes. The scripts and examples support local CSV files with SMILES columns and target property columns.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Some workflows may download MoleculeNet datasets, dependencies, or pretrained model assets through DeepChem or Hugging Face.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning. The reviewed issues are mostly false positives, but scripts should run in a controlled environment.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
